# Vendor Contract — Negotiation Playbook

## Clause: Payment Within 90+ Days (Excessive Credit Period for MSME Vendors)
Act: MSMED Act 2006, Section 15; RBI guidelines
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Payment beyond 45 days to an MSME supplier is illegal. For non-MSME vendors, 90-day or longer credit terms are exploitative and should be negotiated. Courts have upheld MSME interest claims.
Standard counter-clause: "Invoices shall be paid within 30 days of acceptance for MSME-registered Vendors, and within 45 days for all other Vendors. For MSME Vendors, late payment shall attract compound interest at three times the RBI Bank Rate as per Section 16 of the MSMED Act 2006."

## Clause: Client May Withhold Payment for Any Reason
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 55; MSMED Act 2006
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: An unlimited right to withhold payment defeats the vendor's core contractual right. Any withholding right should be tied to specific, documented disputes and should only cover the disputed amount, not the entire invoice.
Standard counter-clause: "The Client may withhold payment only for the specific portion of an invoice that is genuinely disputed, and only after providing written notice detailing the specific dispute within 7 days of invoice receipt. Undisputed portions shall be paid on time. Withheld amounts shall be released within 7 days of dispute resolution."

## Clause: Vendor Bears All Risk of Scope Change
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872; standard construction/IT contract jurisprudence
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Scope creep without a formal change order mechanism and additional compensation is a common exploitation pattern. Courts have held that work materially beyond the original scope entitles the vendor to quantum meruit compensation.
Standard counter-clause: "Any change to the agreed scope, deliverables, or timeline shall be formalized through a written Change Order signed by both parties, specifying the additional cost, timeline impact, and revised acceptance criteria. No additional work shall be commenced without an executed Change Order."

## Clause: Excessive Liquidated Damages (More Than 10% of Contract Value)
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 74; Hadley v Baxendale principle applied by Indian courts
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Indian courts (following Section 74) have consistently reduced liquidated damages clauses that they find to be penal in nature rather than a genuine pre-estimate of loss. LD clauses exceeding 10% of contract value are routinely challenged and reduced.
Standard counter-clause: "Liquidated damages for delay in delivery attributable to the Vendor (excluding Client-caused delays) shall not exceed [specific rate per week, e.g., 0.5%] of the value of the delayed deliverable per week of delay, subject to a maximum cap of 5% of the total contract value. LDs shall be the Client's sole and exclusive remedy for delay."

## Clause: Uncapped Indemnity by Vendor
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 73–74
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: An indemnity clause without any financial cap exposes the vendor to unlimited liability which may be out of proportion to the contract value. Courts have held that disproportionate indemnity obligations can be re-calibrated to a reasonable level.
Standard counter-clause: "The Vendor's total indemnification obligation under this agreement, including IP indemnification, shall not exceed [2-3×] the total fees paid by the Client to the Vendor under this agreement. This cap shall not apply to claims arising from fraud, wilful misconduct, or breach of confidentiality obligations."

## Clause: Client Retains All IP Including Vendor's Pre-Existing Tools
Act: Copyright Act 1957; Indian Contract Act 1872
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Assigning pre-existing vendor IP (tools, frameworks, libraries, methodologies) to the client without compensation is inequitable and often unenforceable. The contract should distinguish between pre-existing IP (retained by vendor) and project-specific deliverables (assigned to client).
Standard counter-clause: "The Client shall own all project-specific deliverables created exclusively for this engagement. The Vendor retains ownership of all pre-existing tools, frameworks, libraries, and methodologies ('Background IP'). The Vendor grants the Client a perpetual, non-exclusive, royalty-free license to use Background IP incorporated in the deliverables for the Client's internal business purposes."

## Clause: Automatic Renewal Without Adequate Notice
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872; general contract principles
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Auto-renewal clauses that trigger with very short opt-out windows (less than 30 days) are exploitative. The vendor or client should be given adequate notice to renegotiate terms before auto-renewal.
Standard counter-clause: "This agreement shall automatically renew for successive one-year terms unless either party provides written notice of non-renewal at least sixty days before the expiry of the then-current term. Any renewal shall be at rates agreed in writing; if no new rates are agreed, existing rates shall apply with a CPI-linked inflation adjustment."

## Clause: Vendor's Employees Deemed Client's Employees (Co-Employment Risk)
Act: Contract Labour (Regulation and Abolition) Act 1970; Supreme Court jurisprudence
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: If the client exercises control over the vendor's workers' daily activities (assigning work, setting hours, directing tasks), Indian courts and labour authorities may deem those workers to be the client's employees, triggering statutory obligations (EPF, ESI, gratuity). The contract should make the vendor-client relationship explicit.
Standard counter-clause: "The Vendor's personnel are employees of the Vendor and not of the Client. The Vendor shall exercise exclusive control over their employment terms, daily supervision, and conduct. This agreement is a service contract and shall not create an employer-employee relationship between the Client and any of the Vendor's personnel. The Vendor shall be solely responsible for all statutory compliances for its personnel."

## Clause: Unilateral Price Escalation by Vendor
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872; relevant commodity price jurisprudence
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: A vendor should not have unchecked right to raise prices during the contract term. Price escalation should be tied to an objective index (WPI, CPI, RBI data) and capped at a reasonable rate. Clients should negotiate price stability clauses.
Standard counter-clause: "The contract price shall remain fixed for the first year of the agreement. Thereafter, price adjustments shall not exceed the change in the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) for the relevant commodity category, as published by the Ministry of Commerce, or 5% per annum, whichever is lower."

## Clause: No Service Level Agreement or Performance Metrics
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 55; general commercial contract principles
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: A vendor agreement without defined SLAs, KPIs, or performance metrics creates ambiguity about what constitutes acceptable performance and makes it nearly impossible to enforce a breach claim. All material service agreements should have measurable performance standards.
Standard counter-clause: "The Vendor shall meet the following minimum service standards: [specific metrics e.g. 99% uptime, 4-hour response time for critical issues, monthly delivery of specified reports]. Monthly SLA performance reports shall be provided by the 5th of the following month. Service credits shall apply for sustained SLA failures as per Schedule B."

## Clause: Confidentiality Obligation Only on Vendor (Not Mutual)
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872; Information Technology Act 2000
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: One-sided confidentiality that only binds the vendor but not the client is inequitable, especially if the vendor shares proprietary methodologies, pricing data, or technical know-how with the client. Mutual NDAs are fair practice.
Standard counter-clause: "Both parties acknowledge that each may receive confidential information of the other party. Both parties agree to maintain the confidentiality of the other's Confidential Information, restrict its use to the purposes of this agreement, and not disclose it to third parties without prior written consent. This mutual obligation shall survive termination for two years."

## Clause: Client Can Assign Agreement Without Vendor Consent
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 37; company law principles
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Assignment of the client's contractual rights and obligations to a third party (e.g., during a merger or acquisition) without the vendor's consent may expose the vendor to dealing with an unknown counterparty. The vendor should require consent for assignment.
Standard counter-clause: "Neither party may assign this agreement or any rights or obligations hereunder without the prior written consent of the other party (not to be unreasonably withheld). In the event of a merger, acquisition, or change of control of the Client, the Vendor may terminate this agreement with 30 days' notice if the acquiring entity is a direct competitor of the Vendor."

## Clause: Penalty for Delay Without Credit for Client-Caused Delays
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 53; construction contract jurisprudence
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Delay penalty clauses should explicitly exclude delays caused by the client's failure to provide approvals, data, access, or resources on time. Applying LD for client-caused delays is unenforceable and courts have consistently relieved vendors from such penalties.
Standard counter-clause: "Liquidated damages for delay shall apply only to delays directly attributable to the Vendor and shall be reduced by the number of days of delay directly caused by: the Client's failure to provide agreed inputs, access, or approvals on schedule; changes to scope; or events of force majeure. Client-caused delays shall extend the delivery timeline by an equivalent number of days without penalty."

## Clause: Sub-Contracting Prohibited Without Carve-Out for Affiliates
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872; Competition Act 2002
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: A blanket prohibition on sub-contracting may be commercially impractical for large vendors. The contract should permit sub-contracting to affiliates and pre-approved partners, while maintaining the vendor's prime responsibility for the work quality and compliance.
Standard counter-clause: "The Vendor may sub-contract portions of the work to its group companies and to pre-approved sub-contractors without requiring Client's consent, provided the Vendor remains primarily liable for all such sub-contracted work. Any sub-contractor not on the pre-approved list requires prior written Client approval. The Vendor shall ensure sub-contractors are bound by equivalent confidentiality and data protection obligations."

## Clause: Termination for Convenience Effective Immediately
Act: Indian Contract Act 1872, Section 73; quantum meruit principles
Contract type: vendor
Requirement: Immediate termination for convenience leaves a vendor with no ability to wind down operations, retrieve personnel, or recover committed costs. A reasonable notice period (proportionate to contract size and duration) is fair and expected in commercial contracts.
Standard counter-clause: "Termination for convenience requires sixty days' written notice (ninety days if the annual contract value exceeds INR 50 lakhs). Upon termination, the Client shall pay for all work performed and reasonable wind-down costs (demobilization, non-cancellable third-party commitments made within the scope of the agreement) incurred by the Vendor within the notice period."
